life_stories.title: The 2008 Portfolio Collapse
life_stories.event: Watched his retirement savings drop 34% in six months during the financial crisis, despite his financial advisor's assurances. He had trusted the advisor completely and felt blindsided by both the market and the betrayal of that confidence.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Crystallized his low risk tolerance (0.32) and high loss aversion (0.76). Now in his 60s, he avoids growth investments entirely and has become deeply skeptical of institutions—particularly financial ones—explaining his low institutional trust across media and government.

life_stories.title: Built the Phoenix Home with His Own Hands
life_stories.event: Spent three years of weekends and evenings helping construct their custom home in Phoenix with a small crew, obsessing over material quality and craftsmanship while staying under budget through sweat equity rather than contractor costs.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Reinforced his primary value driver (quality) and his habit strength (0.76)—he still maintains meticulous routines for home care and refuses to compromise on durability despite budget constraints. This self-reliance also explains his lower trust orientation outside family and his preference for doing things himself rather than delegating.

life_stories.title: Early Retirement Due to Health Concerns
life_stories.event: A cardiac scare and his wife's arthritis diagnosis prompted them both to retire two years earlier than planned, forcing them to recalibrate their fixed income and confront their mortality directly.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Intensified his religious salience (0.58) and his focus on quality of remaining life over accumulation. Created the moderate economic constraint (0.58) that now defines his decision-making, making the tension between quality and budget (his core tension seed) acutely personal. Also deepened his family-first trust orientation as caregiving became central to daily life.

narrative.first_person:
I've learned the hard way that you can't trust everything—especially not Wall Street. When 2008 hit, I watched my nest egg evaporate. That burned me deep. Now I'm sixty-four, living comfortably enough here in Phoenix with my wife, but I'm never careless with money again.

Quality matters to me. I won't buy garbage that falls apart in six months. But I also won't overpay just because a brand says it's premium. I clip coupons. I comparison shop. My wife thinks I'm stubborn about it, but after losing big, being deliberate feels like survival.

I stick with brands I know work—the ones my parents trusted, or companies that have proven themselves over decades. That's real trust. Not slick marketing. I'd rather buy one good thing that lasts than five mediocre ones. It's the tension I live in: wanting the best without throwing away what we've rebuilt.
narrative.third_person:
David Kowalski exemplifies the post-2008 cautious consumer. His 2008 portfolio collapse fundamentally reshaped his financial psychology, embedding loss aversion (0.76) so deeply that he now avoids growth investments entirely and distrusts institutional guidance. This trauma-informed conservatism manifests in meticulous purchase deliberation, where every decision becomes a cost-benefit calculation against his middle-income constraints.

His low institutional trust (0.32) paradoxically strengthens his reliance on family endorsements and familiar brands. Where institutions failed him, trusted relationships and proven products provide psychological safety. This explains his habitual decision-making style (0.27)—repetition of known-good choices reduces decision friction and perceived risk.

David's core tension—valuing quality while constrained by budget—reflects his vulnerability to economic uncertainty. He cannot afford premium goods consistently, yet his quality standard remains high. This manifests as deal-seeking behavior: he's price-sensitive but not indiscriminate. He seeks value equivalence, not mere cheapness.

At 64, approaching retirement, David's psychological profile is fixed. He won't embrace growth-oriented financial thinking or institutional trust. Marketing to David succeeds through familiar brands, transparent pricing, and demonstrated value—not aspirational promises his lived experience has taught him to reject.
